facts_disclosed: Firm shifted focus entirely to heritage restoration — she led the transition
Won contract for Palacio Cousiño annex restoration

turns.content: I cannot stop smiling and it is only 7 in the morning and I have not even finished my coffee. I need to plan a celebration and I need your help thinking through the details
That sounds like a great way to start the morning! What are you celebrating?

so my firm — we have completely shifted our practice to heritage restoration. I have been pushing for this for years and I finally led the transition, brought the partners around, restructured the whole operation. and as if the universe wanted to confirm that this was the right path — we just won the contract for restoring the Palacio Cousiño annex. the annex! that building has been sitting there neglected for decades and now it is ours to bring back to life

That's extraordinary — congratulations. Leading a firm through a complete practice transition is no small thing, and landing a project like the Palacio Cousiño annex as your first major heritage contract is remarkable validation. That building has real historical significance. What kind of celebration are you thinking?

that is what I need help with. I want something that honors the moment without being extravagant — we are architects, not investment bankers. the team has been incredible through this transition, some of them took a real leap of faith following me away from work that was paying well. I owe them something meaningful

That context helps a lot — personal and significant rather than flashy. A few ideas:

1. **Dinner at a heritage venue.** Host it in a restored building — a restaurant in an old Santiago mansion or cultural center. It reinforces the mission.
2. **On-site gathering at the annex itself.** If you can get access, even an informal walk-through with wine and food could be incredibly powerful.
3. **Something commemorative.** Commission a sketch, photograph, or print of the annex in its current state — something each team member can keep and compare to the restored version years from now.

What resonates?

the on-site gathering — yes. that is it. I want them to stand inside the annex and feel what I felt when I first walked through it. the plasterwork is falling but you can still see the original moldings underneath, the ceiling roses, the ironwork on the staircase. it is like peeling back skin and finding the skeleton still strong. we could bring wine and empanadas and just be there together in the space

That sounds perfect — intimate, meaningful, and directly connected to the work. A few practical things:

- **Safety:** If the building is in disrepair, make sure the gathering areas are structurally sound. Hard hats in some sections, good lighting if the electrical isn't functional.
- **Access:** You likely have site access with the contract, but confirm with the municipality that an informal gathering is permitted.
- **Timing:** Late afternoon for natural light through the windows, transitioning into evening. Lanterns or string lights rather than harsh work lamps.
- **Keep it simple:** Wine, empanadas, a short toast. Let the building do the talking.

the building will absolutely do the talking, it always does. I will check with the municipality about access — we have our first formal site assessment scheduled for next week anyway so I know the structural condition of the ground floor is acceptable. the staircase is the concern but we do not need to go upstairs for this
Smart — stay on the ground floor where you're confident in the integrity. That should still have plenty of character. Will you say something to the team, or keep it purely informal?

I think I have to say something. these people believed in me when I said we should leave behind the comfortable work and stake everything on heritage. Sofía left a good position at another firm to join us. Tomás took a pay cut. I want them to know that I see what they sacrificed

Then say exactly that. Name each person individually, acknowledge what they gave up or risked, and connect it to the building standing around you. A toast that's specific and personal will mean far more than a generic thank-you speech. You don't need to be long — just honest.

honest is enough. you are right. I was thinking about how to close the toast and I keep coming back to Darwish — he wrote about how we carry what makes life worth living on this earth. and standing in that annex with my team, about to restore something the world tried to forget — that is what makes life worth living

That's a powerful way to close — connecting the poetry to the physical space and the people. The team will feel it. How many people are you expecting? That affects logistics — food, glasses, lighting.

the core team is seven. Sofía, Tomás, Claudia, Andrés, Matías, Valentina, and me. maybe eight if Diego comes, but I think this should be just us — the ones who built this thing together. he will understand. he always understands

Seven people is perfect for an intimate gathering — small enough that everyone fits in one room and can hear your toast without a microphone. For logistics: one or two bottles of wine, maybe 3-4 dozen empanadas, and you're set. Bring real glasses, not plastic — it matters on an occasion like this.

real glasses, absolutely. I would sooner drink from the floor than from plastic on a night like this. someone on the team makes incredible empanadas de pino so I will ask her to bring those. and I know exactly which Carmenere to bring — I recently got good advice on Chilean wine, actually
Sounds like you've got the essentials covered. When are you thinking — this weekend?

Saturday late afternoon if the access works out. golden hour light coming through whatever windows are still intact. I want them to see the building the way I see it — not as a ruin but as a promise. the way you look at something broken and see everything it is about to become

Golden hour in a heritage building with Carmenere and empanadas and a Darwish toast — your team will remember that evening for years. Get the access confirmed and keep the logistics simple. Congratulations again — leading a firm through that kind of transformation takes real conviction.

conviction, stubbornness, and a grandmother who taught me that the things worth preserving are the things everyone else wants to tear down. oh — one more thing. do you think I should document the gathering? photographs, a short video? or would that break the intimacy

I'd say take a few photos but don't make it a production. Ask one person to take a handful of candid shots — the group standing in the space, the light, the details you want to remember. But don't set up a tripod or make people pose. The best documentation of a moment like this is memory first, photographs second.

you are right. I will ask Claudia, she has a good eye and she is discreet with a camera. a few photographs of the team in the annex before we begin the work — that is all we need. years from now we will look at those photos and remember the building as it was before we touched it. that is a kind of archive too
A before-the-restoration photograph of the team in the space — that's going to be a powerful image to look back on. Enjoy the planning, and enjoy Saturday. You've earned this moment.
thank you for helping me think this through. I am going to finish my coffee now and call the municipality. yalla
Enjoy that coffee. And enjoy Saturday.
